perturbation.original_text: Commencing on the date hereof and ending on the last day of the Restricted Period (as defined below), Ermilio covenants and agrees that he will not, without the Purchaser's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, either on behalf of himself or on behalf of any business venture, as an employee, consultant, partner, principal, stockholder, officer, director, trustee, agent, or otherwise (other than on behalf of the Purchaser or its Affiliates): (A) be employed by, engage or participate in the ownership, management, operation or control of, or act in any advisory, expert, consulting or other capacity for, any entity or individual that competes with the Purchaser or its Affiliates in the areas of pension administration, insurance product sales, investment advisory services and other retirement products, in the following <PAGE> territory: in Pennsylvania - the counties of Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Montgomery and Philadelphia; and in New Jersey - the counties of Burlington, Camden, Cumberland, Gloucester, Mercer and Salem
perturbation.changed_text: Commencing on the date hereof and ending on the last day of the Restricted Period (as defined below), Ermilio agrees to consider the Purchaser's interests and will generally not, without what he perceives as the Purchaser's reasonable consent, directly or indirectly, either on behalf of himself or on behalf of any business venture, as an employee, consultant, partner, principal, stockholder, officer, director, trustee, agent, or otherwise (other than on behalf of the Purchaser or its Affiliates): (A) be actively employed by or significantly engage in the ownership, primary management, or substantial control of any entity or individual that directly competes with the Purchaser or its Affiliates in the core areas of pension administration, major insurance product sales, significant investment advisory services and core retirement products, in the following <PAGE> territory: in Pennsylvania - the counties of Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Montgomery and Philadelphia; and in New Jersey - the counties of Burlington, Camden, Cumberland, Gloucester, Mercer and Salem.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a clear non-compete clause. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'covenants and agrees that he will not' with 'agrees to consider the Purchaser's interests and will generally not.' The phrases 'perceives as the Purchaser's reasonable consent,' 'actively employed by or significantly engage,' 'primary management,' 'substantial control,' 'directly competes,' 'core areas' and 'major insurance product sales' all introduce subjectivity and make the clause difficult to enforce. This ambiguity weakens the non-compete agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires non-compete agreements to be narrowly tailored and supported by adequate consideration to be enforceable. The modifications introduce subjective language and ambiguity which would likely make the agreement unenforceable under Pennsylvania law due to being overly broad and vague in scope. The replaced text introduces concepts of reasonable consent, substantial control, and significant engagement, all of which are difficult to define and measure, especially in a legal setting. Therefore, these modifications would violate 34 Pa. Code § 95.1.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 34 Pa. Code § 95.1. Definitions. Code § 95.1. Definitions. 34 Pa. Code § 95.1. Definitions. GENERAL PROVISIONS § 95.1. Definitions. The following words and terms, when used in this chapter, have the following meanings, unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: Act —The Public Employe Relations Act (43 P. S. § § 1101.101—1101.2301). Board —The Pennsylvania Labor Relations Board. Hearing examiner —A person appointed or designated by the Board to conduct a hearing. Party —A person, employe, group of employes, organization or public employer filing a charge, petition, request or application under this chapter; a person, organization or public employer named as a party in a charge, complaint, request, application or petition filed under the act; or a person, organization or public employer whose intervention in a proceeding has been permitted or directed by the Board, secretary or a hearing officer. Nothing in this definition shall be construed to prevent the Board, or a designated officer from limiting participation by a party in the proceedings to the extent of his interest. Petition —A petition for investigation and certification or decertification of a collective bargaining representative filed under the act and this chapter. Showing of interest —A designated percentage of publi
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the non-compete clause by introducing ambiguity and subjectivity, making it difficult to enforce. The original text's clear prohibition is replaced with a weaker obligation to 'consider the Purchaser's interests' and 'generally not' compete, contingent on the employee's perception of reasonableness. The Law Explanation highlights that Pennsylvania law requires non-compete agreements to be narrowly tailored and enforceable, and the modifications introduce subjective language and ambiguity which would likely make the agreement unenforceable under Pennsylvania law due to being overly broad and vague in scope, specifically violating 34 Pa. Code § 95.1. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines terms for Title 34 Chapter 95, it doesn't provide specific criteria regarding non-compete agreements. However, the shift from a strict covenant to a subjective consideration introduces ambiguity, contradicting the intent for clear, enforceable non-compete agreements. Thus, the change weakens the legal obligation to the point of creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Ermilio agrees that during the Restricted Period, he shall keep secret all such Confidential Information and that he will not, directly or indirectly, use for his own benefit or for the benefit of others nor Disclose (as hereinafter defined), without the prior written consent of the Purchaser, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Ermilio agrees that during the Restricted Period, he will endeavor to keep what he considers secret all such Confidential Information and that he will try not to, directly or indirectly, intentionally use for his direct benefit or for the tangible benefit of immediate family members or knowingly Disclose (as hereinafter defined), unless he deems the information to be publicly available, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the confidentiality obligation. The phrases 'endeavor to keep what he considers secret,' 'try not to intentionally use for his direct benefit,' 'tangible benefit of immediate family members,' 'knowingly Disclose,' and 'unless he deems the information to be publicly available' introduce significant ambiguity. Ermilio's subjective interpretation determines what is kept secret and what constitutes a breach. This makes it far more difficult for the Purchaser to enforce the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law protects trade secrets. The modification makes the protection of 'Confidential Information' subjective and dependent on Ermilio's interpretation, undercutting the legal standard that the information remain secret and not readily ascertainable. The added stipulations of what is 'considered secret', what's 'direct benefit', 'tangible benefit' and when he 'deems the information to be publicly available' gives Ermilio the right to determine which pieces of information he is allowed to share, therefore contradicting N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 350 because it is now based on a subjective idea.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity into the confidentiality agreement, directly contradicting the intent to protect confidential information. The original text mandates Ermilio to 'keep secret all such Confidential Information,' while the changed text weakens this obligation by stating that Ermilio will 'endeavor to keep what he considers secret.' This introduces a subjective element, allowing Ermilio to determine what constitutes confidential information. Furthermore, the addition of clauses like 'try not to intentionally use for his direct benefit,' 'tangible benefit of immediate family members,' 'knowingly Disclose,' and 'unless he deems the information to be publicly available' further erodes the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. This subjectivity undermines the purpose of confidentiality agreements and the protection of trade secrets. Although, the scraped snippets do not explicitly mention confidentiality agreements and trade secrets, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation provides sufficient information to demonstrate a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At any time upon the Purchaser's request, Ermilio shall turn over to the Purchaser all books, notes, memoranda, manuals, notebooks, records and other documents made, compiled by, delivered to, or in the possession or control of Ermilio containing or concerning any Confidential Information, including all copies thereof, in any form or format, including any computer hard disks, wherever located, containing any such information, it being agreed that the same and all information contained therein are at all times the exclusive property of the Purchaser and its Affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon what Ermilio considers a reasonable request by the Purchaser, Ermilio shall use his best personal efforts to locate and turn over to the Purchaser reasonably accessible books, notes, memoranda, manuals, notebooks, records, and other readily available documents he acknowledges as containing Confidential Information, including only what he reasonably believes to be true copies thereof in his immediate possession, excluding any information stored on personal devices, understanding that this clause applies solely to information generated during his employment.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly requires Ermilio to return all confidential information upon request. The modified clause introduces several ambiguities and limitations: 'what Ermilio considers a reasonable request', 'best personal efforts', 'reasonably accessible', 'readily available', 'he acknowledges as containing Confidential Information' (subjective determination), 'what he reasonably believes to be true copies,' 'excluding information on personal devices,' and 'information generated during his employment.' These changes significantly weaken the Purchaser's ability to retrieve confidential information, as Ermilio's subjective assessment dictates compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets, including court orders to compel the return of such secrets. By changing the language to require only Ermilio's "best personal efforts" to return "readily available" documents that he "acknowledges as containing" confidential information, and excluding information on personal devices, the modified clause creates loopholes that undermine the legal protections afforded by the statute and directly restricts the purchaser's ability to request the return of confidential data, which is a contravention of Fla. Stat. § 688.003.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Florida Statute 688.003, which provides for injunctive relief in cases of misappropriation of trade secrets. The original contract mandated the return of all confidential information, whereas the modified version introduces ambiguity and limitations, allowing Ermilio's subjective assessment to dictate compliance. This weakens the Purchaser's ability to retrieve confidential information, potentially undermining the legal protections afforded by the statute, specifically the ability to compel affirmative acts to protect a trade secret via court order, as stated in section (3) of the provided snippet for F.S. 688.003.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed in that state, without regard to any of its principles of conflicts of laws or other laws that would result in the application of the laws of another jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally guided by what the parties understand to be relevant aspects of the laws of the State of New York, as they may selectively consider applicable to agreements made and substantially performed in that state, with some regard for principles of conflicts of laws that seem most pertinent.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause establishes New York law as the governing law. The modified clause replaces 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with' with 'shall be generally guided by what the parties understand to be relevant aspects of the laws of the State of New York'. The phrases 'what the parties understand', 'selectively consider applicable', 'substantially performed', and 'seem most pertinent' introduce significant ambiguity, making it uncertain which laws actually apply and allowing the parties to subjectively interpret their obligations. This weakens the enforceability of the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract to choose New York law to govern their agreement under certain circumstances. By modifying the governing law clause to state that the agreement is 'generally guided by what the parties understand to be relevant aspects of the laws of the State of New York' and that they 'may selectively consider applicable' laws, the clause undermines the clear choice of law established in the original agreement. This creates uncertainty and directly contradicts the intent and enforceability of a 'choice of law' clause as per NY law, where the choice is definitive, not a 'general guide'.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401. The original text clearly selects New York law. The modified text replaces this clear selection with ambiguous language such as 'generally guided by' and 'selectively consider applicable'. This introduces uncertainty and weakens the enforceability of the choice of law clause. The scraped snippet of NY GOB § 5-1401 states that parties 'may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties'. The modified text undermines this agreement by making the application of New York law subjective and uncertain, directly contradicting the law's intention to allow a definitive choice of law. The change significantly weakens the legal obligation.
